message.content:
Below is a curated list of the *canonical, authoritative* sources and standards for **web performance and Core Web Vitals** testing, limited to primary specifications and official Google/W3C/standards documentation.

For each entry I include: **Name, Organization, What it defines (metrics/categories), Canonical URL**.

---

### 1. Google Lighthouse – Performance Audits

- **Name**: Lighthouse Performance category & audits  
- **Organization**: Google (Chrome / Chrome DevTools team)  
- **Defines**:
  - Overall **Performance score** based on lab metrics including **First Contentful Paint (FCP)**, **Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)**, **Speed Index (SI)**, **Total Blocking Time (TBT)**, **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)**, and **Time to First Byte (TTFB)**.[9]
  - A set of **performance audits** (each with an audit ID) covering:
    - Render‑blocking resources, unused CSS/JS, image optimization, text compression, caching, preconnect/preload hints, main‑thread work, JavaScript execution, etc. (e.g. audits like `render-blocking-resources`, `uses-optimized-images`, `uses-text-compression`, `uses-long-cache-ttl`, `total-byte-weight`, `unminified-css`, `unminified-javascript`, `bootup-time`, `long-tasks`, etc.)[9]
  - Used as the engine behind PageSpeed Insights lab data and DevTools Lighthouse panel.  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - Lighthouse Performance category & metric definitions:  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/performance/`  
  - Lighthouse overview:  
    `https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/`

*(Note: Individual audit IDs are documented in the Lighthouse docs; when implementing or mapping, use each audit’s page under the Performance section.)*

---

### 2. Web Vitals & Core Web Vitals (web.dev)

- **Name**: Web Vitals / Core Web Vitals  
- **Organization**: Google (web.dev & Chrome)  
- **Defines**:
  - **Web Vitals** program and **Core Web Vitals** metrics:  
    - **Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)** – loading performance.  
    - **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)** – visual stability.  
    - **Interaction to Next Paint (INP)** – responsiveness/interactivity.[9]  
  - Recommended **good/needs improvement/poor thresholds** and guidance on field vs lab measurement.[9]  
  - Distinguishes **Core Web Vitals** from other Web Vitals (e.g., FCP, TTFB).  
  - Shows how to measure via the **web-vitals JS library**, RUM, Lighthouse, and Chrome UX Report (CrUX).[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - Web Vitals (Core Web Vitals explanation & metrics):  
    `https://web.dev/articles/vitals`[9]

---

### 3. Core Web Vitals in Google Search

- **Name**: Understanding Core Web Vitals and Google search results  
- **Organization**: Google Search Central  
- **Defines**:
  - How **Core Web Vitals** (LCP, INP, CLS) are used in **Google Search ranking** as part of page experience.[8]  
  - Confirms that Core Web Vitals are based on **real‑world (field) data** from Chrome users.[8]  
  - Thresholds for “good” user experience and how Search Console / PageSpeed Insights reflect these metrics.[8]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/core-web-vitals`[8]

---

### 4. Chrome UX Report (CrUX)

- **Name**: Chrome UX Report (CrUX)  
- **Organization**: Google (Chrome)  
- **Defines**:
  - A **public field‑data dataset** of real‑user experience metrics, including **Core Web Vitals** (LCP, INP, CLS) and supporting metrics like FCP and TTFB, collected from opted‑in Chrome users.[9][8]  
  - Used as the **source of field data** for PageSpeed Insights, Search Console Core Web Vitals report, and BigQuery/CrUX API analysis.[8][9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - CrUX documentation on web.dev:  
    `https://web.dev/articles/chrome-ux-report` (accessible via web.dev docs that describe CrUX and how it feeds Core Web Vitals data)[9]

*(CrUX is referenced as the canonical field-data source for Core Web Vitals in Google’s own docs.[8][9])*

---

### 5. Google RAIL Performance Model

- **Name**: RAIL – Response, Animation, Idle, Load performance model  
- **Organization**: Google (Chrome / web.dev)  
- **Defines**:
  - A **user‑centric performance model** specifying targets for:
    - **Response**: input → response in under 100 ms.  
    - **Animation & Scroll**: frames in 10 ms (~60 fps).  
    - **Idle**: perform work in idle time without hurting responsiveness.  
    - **Load**: content becomes interactive quickly (perceived load bounds).[9]  
  - Used as a conceptual framework behind metrics like FID/INP, LCP, etc., and for setting performance budgets and thresholds.  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - RAIL performance model article:  
    `https://web.dev/articles/rail` (linked and discussed from the Web Vitals and performance guidance on web.dev)[9]

---

### 6. web.dev Performance Learning Path

- **Name**: web.dev Performance learning path  
- **Organization**: Google (web.dev)  
- **Defines**:
  - A curated **curriculum of performance guidance** including:
    - Loading performance (network, resource loading, compression, caching).  
    - Rendering and main‑thread performance.  
    - Core Web Vitals optimization.  
    - Measurement and monitoring practices (Lighthouse, CrUX, RUM).[9]  
  - Not a formal standard, but the **canonical educational reference** from Google for modern web performance and Web Vitals.  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - Performance learning path (Performance landing page on web.dev):  
    `https://web.dev/learn/performance/` (reachable from web.dev Learn section that covers performance best practices referenced in Web Vitals docs)[9]

---

### 7. W3C Navigation Timing

- **Name**: Navigation Timing (Level 1 / Level 2)  
- **Organization**: W3C Web Performance Working Group  
- **Defines**:
  - The **Navigation Timing API**, providing high‑resolution timestamps for key page load milestones (redirect Start/End, DNS, TCP connect, request/response, DOMContentLoaded, load event) via `PerformanceNavigationTiming`.  
  - Underpins metrics like **Time To First Byte (TTFB)** and other derived load timing metrics used in tools such as Lighthouse and RUM libraries.[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:
  - Navigation Timing Level 2 Recommendation:  
    `https://www.w3.org/TR/navigation-timing-2/`

---

### 8. W3C Resource Timing

- **Name**: Resource Timing (Level 1 / Level 2)  
- **Organization**: W3C Web Performance Working Group  
- **Defines**:
  - The **Resource Timing API** that exposes detailed timing for subresources (scripts, CSS, images, fonts, XHR/fetch, etc.) via `PerformanceResourceTiming`.  
  - Enables measurement of **resource load performance**, critical for audits on render‑blocking resources, image/JS/CSS loading behavior, and calculating derived performance budgets.[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  `https://www.w3.org/TR/resource-timing-2/`

---

### 9. W3C Paint Timing

- **Name**: Paint Timing  
- **Organization**: W3C Web Performance Working Group  
- **Defines**:
  - The **Paint Timing API**, providing `first-paint` and **First Contentful Paint (FCP)** entries via `PerformancePaintTiming`.  
  - FCP is a **Web Vital** (non‑core) and used in Lighthouse Performance scoring and some Web Vitals guidance.[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  `https://www.w3.org/TR/paint-timing/`

---

### 10. W3C Long Tasks

- **Name**: Long Tasks API  
- **Organization**: W3C Web Performance Working Group  
- **Defines**:
  - The **Long Tasks API**, which reports tasks on the main thread that exceed 50 ms via `PerformanceLongTaskTiming`.  
  - Foundation for derived metrics like **Total Blocking Time (TBT)** in Lighthouse and contributes to understanding **INP** behavior and responsiveness issues.[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  `https://www.w3.org/TR/longtasks/`

---

### 11. Largest Contentful Paint API

- **Name**: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) API  
- **Organization**: W3C Web Performance Working Group (spec hosted via WICG / WHATWG but referenced by web.dev as the LCP API)  
- **Defines**:
  - The **Largest Contentful Paint** performance entry type (`LargestContentfulPaint`) available from `PerformanceObserver`.  
  - Defines how browsers determine the “largest” content element and when to report LCP, which is the canonical definition underlying the **Core Web Vital LCP** metric.[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - LCP spec:  
    `https://www.w3.org/TR/largest-contentful-paint/` (referenced by web.dev Web Vitals article when describing how to measure LCP via the LCP API)[9]

---

### 12. Layout Instability API (Cumulative Layout Shift)

- **Name**: Layout Instability API (for CLS)  
- **Organization**: W3C Web Performance Working Group (specification developed in Web Incubator/Performance groups)  
- **Defines**:
  - The **Layout Instability API**, which surfaces layout shift events via `LayoutShift` entries.  
  - Provides the underlying event-level data used to compute **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)**, the Core Web Vital for **visual stability**.[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - Layout Instability / CLS spec:  
    `https://www.w3.org/TR/layout-instability/` (linked by Web Vitals docs as the underlying API for CLS measurement)[9]

---

### 13. Event Timing / Event Timing API (basis for INP & legacy FID)

- **Name**: Event Timing (Event Timing API)  
- **Organization**: W3C Web Performance Working Group / WICG  
- **Defines**:
  - The **Event Timing API**, providing timing entries for discrete user interactions (e.g., click, key, tap) used to measure UI responsiveness.  
  - Forms the underlying mechanism for metrics like **First Input Delay (FID)** and the newer **Interaction to Next Paint (INP)** Core Web Vital, via events observed and aggregated by libraries such as `web-vitals`.[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - Event Timing spec:  
    `https://www.w3.org/TR/event-timing/` (referenced by Web Vitals documentation as the underlying interaction timing API)[9]

---

### 14. Resource Hints (Preload, Preconnect, Prefetch, DNS Prefetch)

- **Name**: Resource Hints  
- **Organization**: W3C (W3C Recommendation)  
- **Defines**:
  - HTML **resource hints** such as `<link rel="preload">`, `<link rel="preconnect">`, `<link rel="dns-prefetch">`, `<link rel="prefetch">`.  
  - These affect **loading performance**, and Lighthouse includes audits checking appropriate use of preconnect/preload.[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  `https://www.w3.org/TR/resource-hints/`

---

### 15. HTTP Caching (RFC 9111 and related)

- **Name**: HTTP Caching (HTTP Semantics – Caching)  
- **Organization**: IETF (HTTP Working Group)  
- **Defines**:
  - Core **HTTP caching semantics**: `Cache-Control`, `ETag`, `Expires`, validators, cache revalidation, and freshness rules, now consolidated primarily in **RFC 9111** (HTTP Caching).  
  - Underpins performance best practices and Lighthouse audits like `uses-long-cache-ttl` and `uses-http2`/connection reuse behavior from HTTP semantics.[9]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - HTTP Caching (RFC 9111):  
    `https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111`

---

### 16. HTTP Compression (Content-Encoding / Transfer-Encoding)

- **Name**: HTTP Semantics – Content Coding and Message Transfer (gzip, br, etc.)  
- **Organization**: IETF (HTTP Working Group)  
- **Defines**:
  - **Content codings** like `gzip`, `br` (Brotli), `deflate` and their use via `Content-Encoding` and `Accept-Encoding` headers in HTTP.[9]  
  - Basis for performance best practices and Lighthouse’s `uses-text-compression` audit, which checks whether textual resources are served compressed.  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  - HTTP Semantics (Content codings section, RFC 9110):  
    `https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110`

---

### 17. HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 (Transport Efficiency & Multiplexing)

- **Name**: HTTP/2 and HTTP/3  
- **Organization**: IETF  
- **Defines**:
  - Protocol features (multiplexing, header compression, stream prioritization) that directly affect **resource loading performance**.  
  - Lighthouse includes checks such as using HTTP/2 for static assets (e.g., `uses-http2`).[9]  
- **Canonical URLs**:  
  - HTTP/2: `https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7540`  
  - HTTP/3: `https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9114`

---

### 18. Google Search Console – Core Web Vitals Report

- **Name**: Core Web Vitals report (Search Console)  
- **Organization**: Google Search Central  
- **Defines**:
  - How Google **aggregates CrUX field data** into URL/page-group level **Core Web Vitals statuses** (Good/Needs improvement/Poor).[4][8]  
  - Official thresholds, how to interpret **LCP, INP, CLS** status, and guidance on prioritizing fixes.[4]  
  - Connects to external testing tools such as **Lighthouse** for verification and debugging.[4]  
- **Canonical URL**:  
  `https://support.google.com/webmasters/answer/9205520`[4]

---

These sources collectively constitute the **authoritative foundation** for:

- Defining **Core Web Vitals** and related Web Vitals metrics.  
- Specifying the **underlying browser performance APIs** used by RUM and lab tools.  
- Establishing **HTTP and resource‑level standards** that directly affect performance (caching, compression, hints).  
- Providing the **canonical implementation and audit model** (Lighthouse) used across Google’s performance tooling ecosystem.
